title: The Counselor
description: Read each dilemma and choose the advice or rule with the correct modal.

questions.question: Your friend feels very sick. You say: "You ___ see a doctor."
questions.explanation: "Should" gives advice: "You should see a doctor."

questions.question: You ___ park here. It's a no-parking zone. (prohibition)
There's no food at home. I ___ go shopping.
questions.explanation: "Have to" = external obligation: "I have to go shopping" (there's no food).

questions.question: "___ I use your pen?" (polite request)
questions.explanation: "May I...?" is the polite/formal way to ask permission.

questions.question: It ___ rain later. Take an umbrella just in case.
You ___ ___ ___ wear a uniform. It's optional. (not necessary)
questions.explanation: "Don't have to" = not necessary, not obligatory.

questions.question: Students ___ cheat on exams. It's strictly prohibited.
She ___ speak three languages fluently.
questions.explanation: "Can" expresses ability: "She can speak three languages."

questions.question: You look tired. You ___ get more sleep.
questions.explanation: "Should" for advice: "You should get more sleep."

questions.question: I ___ study harder. I really want to pass the exam. (personal obligation)
questions.explanation: "Must" for personal/internal obligation: "I must study harder."

questions.question: When I was young, I ___ run very fast.
questions.explanation: "Could" = past ability: "I could run fast when I was young."

questions.question: "You ___ be quiet in the library." (rule)
questions.explanation: "Must" for strong rules: "You must be quiet in the library."

title: Reglas y Consejos
description: Build sentences with the correct modal for each situation.

sentences.correct: You must be quiet in the library
I have to wake up early tomorrow
You don't have to come tomorrow
He could run very fast when he was young

title: Modal → Function
description: Match each modal with its main communicative function.

title: Diferencias Sutiles
description: Master contrasts between modals that seem similar.

cards.back:
MUST = personal/internal obligation
HAVE TO = external obligation
cards.example: I must study harder (my decision). I have to wear a uniform (company rule).

cards.back:
MUSTN'T = prohibited, forbidden
DON'T HAVE TO = not necessary, optional
cards.example: You mustn't cheat (prohibited). You don't have to come (it's optional).

cards.back:
CAN = informal permission
MAY = formal/polite permission
cards.example: Can I sit here? (casual) May I come in? (formal)

cards.back:
SHOULD = advice, recommendation
MUST = strong obligation
cards.example: You should exercise more (advice). You must stop at a red light (obligation).

cards.back:
MIGHT = less likely possibility
MAY = more likely possibility
cards.example: It might snow (unlikely). It may rain (more likely).

cards.back:
CAN = present ability
COULD = past ability or polite request
cards.example: I can swim (now). I could swim when I was 5 (past). Could you help me? (polite)

cards.back:
Both express necessity.
HAVE TO = stronger, more common
NEED TO = slightly softer
cards.example: I have to finish this by Monday. I need to call my mom.

cards.back:
Both give advice. Same meaning.
SHOULD = more common in speech
OUGHT TO = slightly more formal

CAN'T = not allowed / impossible
MUSTN'T = prohibited (stronger)
cards.example: You can't park here (not allowed). You mustn't touch that wire (dangerous!).

cards.back:
MAY = formal contexts, writing
CAN = everyday speech
cards.example: May I be excused? (formal) Can I go now? (informal)
